prompt: It is 2026. kolm.ai sells signed, offline-verifiable SECURITY EVIDENCE for AI AGENTS: an AI-agent vendor whose enterprise deal stalls in the buyer's security review uploads the agent's logs, and kolm runs a deterministic audit across 8 controls (least privilege, audit trail, data egress, prompt-injection/red-team, model provenance, evidence, memory/retrieval, multi-agent delegation), maps every finding to SOC 2 / ISO 42001 / NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / OWASP LLM Top 10 / MITRE ATLAS, and produces an Ed25519-SIGNED report the buyer verifies OFFLINE against the vendor's public key with no kolm account and no kolm server in the trust path. Turns a 4-8 week review into days. The current homepage HEADLINE is: 'Security evidence for your AI agent, signed and verifiable.' Critique this headline hard for a technical B2B security buyer. Is it the clearest, most compelling framing, or generic? Propose 3-5 SHARPER alternative headlines (write the actual lines). Then pick the single best and explain why. Be specific and current; cite real 2026 examples of strong security/devtools positioning (@handle or URL + date) you are drawing from.

2026 market language for AI-AGENT SECURITY, TRUST, and ATTESTATION. How do practitioners, founders, CISOs, and AI-vendor sellers actually TALK on X and the web in 2026 about: getting an AI agent through an enterprise security review / procurement; agent identity and provenance; verifiable evidence vs questionnaires; 'agent trust', 'agent passport', 'agent attestation', 'AI BOM', 'signed evidence'. Quote real recent posts/phrases (@handle + date) and real product copy (URLs). Which words/frames RESONATE and convert, which are overused cliches to avoid, and what vocabulary is emerging that a signed-agent-evidence product should adopt to sound native in 2026 without sounding like hype. Conclude with a short glossary of the 8-12 highest-signal terms kolm should use.

For a technical B2B security-evidence product (kolm: signed, offline-verifiable evidence that clears an AI agent's enterprise security review), should the HERO lead with the MECHANISM (cryptographically signed, offline-verifiable, Ed25519, no server in trust path), the OUTCOME (clear the security review in days, not weeks; close the enterprise deal), or the PAIN (your agent deal is stalled in a 6-8 week security review)? Pull evidence from how the best 2026 security/devtools/compliance companies structure their hero (cite Vanta, Drata, Secureframe, Stytch, WorkOS, Vercel, Tailscale, Sigstore, or similar with URLs). What converts best for the specific buyer here (an AI-agent vendor founder/eng-lead selling into the enterprise)? Give a recommended hero structure: exact eyebrow + headline + subhead + primary CTA, and say what to put above the fold next to it.

Map the 2026 competitive landscape kolm sits in and find the defensible white space. Players to assess (current positioning + tagline, cite URLs/@handle+date): Vanta (and its AI/Agentic Trust push), Drata, Secureframe, Lakera, Protect AI, HiddenLayer, Credo AI, Cranium, plus any 'agent identity / agent passport / agent attestation' entrants (e.g. Workday Agent Passport, SPIFFE/SPIRE-based, AI-trust-center products). For each: the one positioning they own. Then: what positioning can kolm OWN that none of them can easily copy, given kolm's unique asset = a vendor-NEUTRAL, OFFLINE-verifiable signed evidence artifact the buyer checks themselves (the 'Agent Identity Passport'). Is the strongest wedge (a) 'signed/verifiable agent security evidence', (b) 'the Agent Identity Passport', (c) 'the SOC 2 for AI agents', or (d) something else? Recommend the single most defensible + searchable wedge and the category name kolm should try to own.

Play a skeptical enterprise security reviewer AND a skeptical seed investor evaluating kolm.ai (signed, offline-verifiable security evidence for AI agents that clears the enterprise review). List the 8 hardest objections / credibility gaps each would raise about the POSITIONING and the claims (e.g. 'a vendor pays you to audit their own agent, so why would a buyer trust it', 'you map to SOC 2 but are not a certification', 'injection is tested not warranted', 'is this a feature not a company', 'why not just use the buyer's existing GRC tool'). For EACH objection, give the strongest honest rebuttal kolm can put ON THE SITE, and flag any objection where the positioning is genuinely weak and should change. Conclude with the 3 trust signals kolm most needs to add to the site to close the credibility gap.

Evaluate kolm's pricing/packaging framing for an AI-agent vendor buyer in 2026. Tiers: free Scan (watermarked preview), $750 one-time Signed Readiness Report, Continuous $299/mo (re-attested weekly) and $999/mo (re-attested every deploy + injection regression), $15k Full Readiness (guided engagement), $25k Reviewed Attestation (named human co-signer). Is the free-scan -> one-time -> continuous -> high-touch ladder the right shape? Is differentiating the two Continuous tiers on CADENCE (weekly vs every-deploy) + DEPTH the right move (we just removed an 'up to 3 vs 15 agents' meter)? Compare to how 2026 security/devtools companies package self-serve + sales-assisted (cite real pricing pages/URLs). Recommend any changes to the FRAMING/labels/order (NOT the locked prices) that would convert better, and the one-line value statement to put atop the pricing page.

Synthesis for kolm.ai's 2026 launch positioning. Given kolm = signed, offline-verifiable security evidence for AI agents (the buyer verifies a vendor's agent-security report themselves, no account, no server in the trust path; the wedge is a vendor-neutral 'Agent Identity Passport'), and the current site says hero 'Security evidence for your AI agent, signed and verifiable' with the 'maps to SOC 2/ISO 42001/NIST/EU AI Act/OWASP/MITRE' framing: is this TRULY the best positioning, or should it change? Give a decisive verdict (KEEP / TIGHTEN / REPLACE) with the exact recommended hero eyebrow + headline + subhead, the one-sentence category description, the single ownable wedge, and the top 5 concrete copy/positioning changes ranked by impact. Be opinionated and specific; ground every recommendation in 2026 market reality with citations.
